text:
Drag queens prepare to perform on the Debilly Bridge in Paris, during the opening ceremony of the 2024 Summer Olympics, Friday, July 26, 2024. A storm of outrage about the Paris Olympics’ opening ceremony took a legal turn Tuesday July 30, 2024, with a DJ who performed at the show saying her lawyer is filing complaints over a torrent of threats and other abuse that the LGBTQ+ icon has suffered online in the ceremony’s wake. (Source: AP Photo/Tsvangirayi Mukwazhi, File)

BLESSINGS: Archbishop Jason Gordon blesses the congregation yesterday during Easter Mass at the Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception, Port of Spain.

—Photo: JERMAINE CRUICKSHANK

ARCHBISHOP Jason Gordon has said that today's culture appears to be 'against' Christian tradition, as he deemed last Friday's opening ceremony of the 2024 Olympic Games to be a 'parody' of Jesus Christ's Last Supper.

The Archbishop joined other local and international religious leaders in criticising the opening ceremony of the world's mostanticipated multi-sporting event, which some claim was a mockery of Christianity and a declaration of war on Christian values.

The opening, hosted in Paris, France, generated claims that one scene formed by actors, including drag queens, was an obvious mockery of Leonardo Da Vinci's famous painting of The Last Supper. Many have also claimed that the ceremony depicted the horseman of death, of the Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse.

The ceremony has been defended as drawing from European history, with the controversial tableau being clarified as a depiction of scenes around the Greek god, Dionysus.

Speaking at Sunday's mass at the Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception in Port of Spain, the Archbishop said the Olympics were supposed to 'display the cultural values of a civilisation'.

Gordon said the Olympic Games were also 'supposed to be that one place where all people are welcome and all people are held together in harmony'. 'It is supposed to be an apolitical place, where there is neutrality and a place where there is a welcome to all people and all cultures. Every time there is an Olympics, you see the cultural creativity of that nation coming to the fore,' he stated.

Tableau parody

Gordon went on to state that the opening ceremony would usually feature 'artistry like you don't see anywhere else'.

He said all of that was true of the Paris Olympics, which showed artistry 'in ways that were mind-blowing, as only the French can do'.

'And then in the midst of this, they had this tableau, which was clearly a depiction of Da Vinci's Last Supper, with drag queens and the like parading as The Last Supper and that was a parody,' Gordon said.

The Archbishop noted the context of The Last Supper, which was that 'Jesus was about to be taken to be whipped, to be mocked, to be handed over, ridiculed and to be given over'.

'That is the context of The Last Supper and that context is back with us today,' Gordon said.

He went on to question whether today's culture was against Christianity, stating that his grandparents' generation 'lived in a culture that could have assumed Christianity as the foundation of the culture'.

'Today, we are living in a culture where we can assume that the culture is against the Christian tradition,' Gordon stated, adding: 'When you make fun of a billion people in an Olympics, when you ridicule what is most sacred to them in an Olympics, there is something fundamentally wrong.'

Seventh-Day Adventist Pastor Clive Dottin also spoke against the opening ceremony, stating in a Facebook post on Sunday:

'You have desecrated the painting of The Last Supper, you have staged a blasphemous mockery of Jesus Christ and his disciples. With insulting impertinence, you have sought to elevate the profane above the sacred. For this ungodly act, you will pay a huge price.'

Dottin's statements were directed at 'French liberals' and the International Olympic Committee (IOC).

title: Archbishop, Dottin: Olympic ceremony mocked Christianity | News Extra | trinidadexpress.com
description: ARCHBISHOP Jason Gordon has said that today's culture appears to be 'against' Christian tradition, as he deemed last Friday's opening ceremony of the 2024 Olympic Games to be a 'parody'
